does: This paper introduces AgentDojo, a dynamic evaluation framework designed to measure the adversarial robustness of large language model (LLM) agents that interact with external tools and untrusted data. The framework is not a static benchmark but an extensible environment that enables researchers to design, implement, and evaluate new agent tasks, defenses, and adaptive attacks. AgentDojo includes 97 realistic tasks spanning email management, e-banking, and travel bookings, along with 629 security test cases and multiple attack and defense paradigms from existing literature. The authors conduct empirical evaluations showing that state-of-the-art LLMs struggle with many tasks even without attacks, and that current prompt injection attacks successfully compromise some but not all security properties, demonstrating that both offensive and defensive techniques have significant room for improvement.
connects: This work builds on the growing concern about prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM-based systems, extending prior research on adversarial attacks against language models to the specific context of agentic AI systems that call external tools. It responds to earlier work on prompt injection attacks and defenses by recognizing that static benchmarks cannot keep pace with rapidly evolving attack and defense strategies. The paper connects to broader research on evaluating AI safety and robustness, particularly the challenge of measuring security properties in systems combining reasoning and tool use. It also draws from established practices in security evaluation where test suites and adversarial frameworks help identify vulnerabilities, but adapts these methodologies for the unique challenges posed by LLMs interacting with untrusted external data sources.
enables: AgentDojo provides an infrastructure for the AI safety and security community to develop and test the next generation of defenses against prompt injection attacks in agentic systems. The extensible nature of the framework enables researchers to add new tasks reflecting emerging real-world scenarios, design more sophisticated attack strategies that adapt to defenses, and evaluate novel mitigation techniques. This work points toward developing more robust agents that can reliably interact with untrusted data sources, which is critical for deploying AI agents in security-sensitive domains. The framework also enables research into fundamental questions about how to design agents that maintain instruction fidelity despite adversarial inputs, potentially informing broader work on AI alignment and control of autonomous systems operating in adversarial environments.
